misconception: Higher alcohol automatically means fuller body and higher quality.
why_incorrect: Alcohol does contribute to body — it increases viscosity and gives a warming sensation. However, high alcohol without sufficient fruit concentration, acidity, or tannin structure produces an unbalanced wine that WSET assessment would penalise. Alcohol above ~15% ABV can appear 'hot' or 'burning' if not balanced. Many of the most complex, highly regarded wines in the world (Grand Cru Mosel Riesling Auslese at 8% ABV, Champagne at 12%) have relatively low alcohol. The SAT assesses alcohol as a structural element, not as a quality indicator in isolation.
corrected_understanding: Alcohol contributes to body and texture, but quality requires balance. High alcohol in an unbalanced wine is a structural fault. Low alcohol in a balanced, complex wine is not a defect.
detection_signals: higher alcohol means fuller body and better quality
doesn't more alcohol mean a more powerful wine
if the alcohol is higher it must be a better wine
this wine has 15% so it must have more body than the 12% one
I scored it higher because the alcohol was higher

coaching_content.confusion_statement: You seem to be confusing: Higher alcohol automatically means fuller body and higher quality.
coaching_content.evidence_statement: This is based on your answers showing this pattern.
coaching_content.improvement_signal: Correctly explaining the relationship in similar questions
